name: Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular trail running in the Sierra de Andujar natural park
name_original: Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular trailrunning por el parque natural de la Sierra de Andújar
region_intro: Jogging around National Park Sierra de Andújar offers diverse landscapes within the Sierra Morena, characterized by gently rolling hills in the south and rocky crags in the north. The region features extensive Mediterranean forests of holm oaks and cork oaks, providing shaded routes. Rivers such as the Yeguas and Jándula, along with reservoirs like Embalse del Encinarejo, create scenic running opportunities along their perimeters. Varied terrain, including open grassy areas and ravines, contributes to a dynamic running environment. Best jogging routes around National Park Sierra de Andújar The most popular jogging route is Sierra Morena Trail: Santuario de la Cabeza Loop, a 18.3 miles (29.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 19 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers a challenging loop around the Santuario de la Cabeza. Another top favourite among local runners is Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular trail running in the Sierra de Andujar natural park, a difficult 16.0 miles (25.7 km) path. This circular route explores the natural park around the Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza. Local runners also love the From Camino Viejo to Ermita de San Ginés — circular through Parque Natura Sierra de Andújar, a 9.6 miles (15.5 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 9 minutes. Jogging around National Park Sierra de Andújar is defined by Mediterranean forests, rolling hills, and river valleys. The extensive network of routes offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging trails. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore National Park Sierra de Andújar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running routes in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: The park offers a diverse landscape for runners. You'll find gently rolling hills in the southern sections, transitioning to more rocky crags in the north. Routes often traverse extensive Mediterranean forests of holm oaks, cork oaks, and pine groves, providing shaded paths. You can also expect to run along rivers like the Yeguas and Jándula, and around reservoirs such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2862464" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, offering varied scenery.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running trails in the National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: Yes, while many routes are moderate to difficult, there are options suitable for beginners. The park features paths with gentler inclines and less technical terrain. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1303794357/embalse-del-encinarejo-by-the-rio-jandula-circular-by-the-parque-natura-sierra-de-andujar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Encinarejo by the Río Jándula — circular by the Parque Natura Sierra de Andújar&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that offers scenic views around the reservoir, suitable for those looking for a less strenuous run.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features I might see while running in the park?
A: Runners can discover several interesting points. The historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1279995" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Cabeza&lt;/a&gt; is a prominent landmark, offering spectacular views. You might also encounter the peaceful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4484492" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Ginés Hermitage&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the tranquility of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2862464" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. Various viewpoints, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5061639" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valley Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1278398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Pastores Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, provide stunning panoramas of the Sierra.

Q: Are there many circular running routes available in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: Yes, the park offers a good selection of circular routes, perfect for varied running experiences. A popular choice is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1301695292/sierra-morena-trail-santuario-de-la-cabeza-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Morena Trail: Santuario de la Cabeza Loop&lt;/a&gt;. Another excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989787073/santuario-virgen-de-la-cabeza-circular-trail-running-in-the-sierra-de-andujar-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular trail running in the Sierra de Andujar natural park&lt;/a&gt;, which explores the natural park around the sanctuary.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: The Mediterranean climate means spring and autumn are generally ideal for running, with milder temperatures and lush landscapes. The aromatic plants like thyme and rosemary are particularly vibrant during these seasons. While summer can be hot, early mornings or late evenings offer cooler conditions. Winter running is also possible, especially in the southern, lower-altitude areas, though some higher, rockier sections might be more exposed.

Q: Are there any long-distance or challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: Absolutely. The National Park Sierra de Andújar is home to several challenging and longer routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1301695292/sierra-morena-trail-santuario-de-la-cabeza-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Morena Trail: Santuario de la Cabeza Loop&lt;/a&gt;, at over 18 miles, is a difficult route offering significant elevation changes. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40310005" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Ginés Hermitage – La Carreta on the Old Road loop from Santuario de la Virgen de la Cabeza&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 21 km with substantial climbs.

Q: What do other runners say about their experience jogging in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: The running routes in National Park Sierra de Andújar are highly regarded by the komoot community. With over 400 runners having explored the area, the routes are praised for their diverse landscapes, from Mediterranean forests to river valleys, and the extensive network of trails that cater to various ability levels. Users often highlight the natural beauty and the sense of untouched wilderness.

Q: Is it possible to find parking near the trailheads for running routes?
A: Yes, the park generally provides access to trailheads, and visitor centers like Centro de Visitantes Las Viñas de Peñallana can offer information on specific parking areas. Many popular routes, especially those leading to significant landmarks like the Sanctuary of the Virgen de la Cabeza, have designated parking facilities nearby to accommodate visitors.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running options in the park?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' routes are not explicitly detailed for running, the park's extensive network includes many paths suitable for walking and hiking that can be adapted for family jogs. Look for routes described as 'easy' or 'moderate' with relatively flat sections, such as those along the Jándula river or around the reservoirs, which offer pleasant and less strenuous experiences for families.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: National parks often have regulations regarding pets to protect wildlife, especially endangered species like the Iberian lynx. While many trails are generally accessible, it's always best to check the specific rules of the National Park Sierra de Andújar regarding dogs, particularly if they need to be on a leash or if there are restricted areas. Information can usually be found at visitor centers.

Q: How many running routes are available in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: There are over 70 running routes available in National Park Sierra de Andújar, offering a wide range of options for all skill levels. This includes 5 easy routes, 40 moderate routes, and 34 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for every runner.

Q: Where can I find more information about the National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: For additional information about the park's natural features, activities, and visitor services, you can visit the official tourism website for the region: &lt;a href="https://www.visit-andalucia.com/sierra-de-andujar-jaen/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;visit-andalucia.com&lt;/a&gt;. This resource can provide further context on the park's Mediterranean ecosystem and its significance.
tips: The Basilica of Nuestra Señora de la Cabeza, located on a promontory from which you can see the Sierra de Andújar, is the second most visited Andalusian sanctuary after the hermitage of El Rocío.
It is a trail that is located within the Sierra de Andújar Natural Park, and is not open to the public
